---
title: "Session 11 · Simple Logistic Regression"
---

## Overview

What happens when the outcome is yes/no rather than a number on a continuous scale? This session opens the second half of the course by extending regression to a binary response. We cannot model a probability with a straight line, so we introduce the logit link, which lets a single predictor act on the log-odds of the outcome. Along the way we meet odds and odds ratios --- the natural currency of logistic regression --- continuing the "specific → general" arc as the linear model generalizes to a new family of outcomes.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Explain why a linear model is ill-suited to a binary outcome.
- Define odds and the logit (log-odds) transformation of a probability.
- Fit a simple logistic regression with a single predictor in R.
- Interpret the slope as a change in log-odds and exponentiate it into an odds ratio.
- Convert fitted log-odds back into predicted probabilities.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 9: Simple Logistic Regression](/notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Lab:** [Lab 9: Simple Logistic Regression](/labs/labs/lab09-logistic-intro.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa clipboard-list >}} **Project:** [Project 1 — Final Report due](/assignment/project-1-final.html)

## Key concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Binary outcome** | A response variable with two possible values, such as success/failure or yes/no. |
| **Odds** | The ratio of the probability of an event to the probability of its complement. |
| **Logit (log-odds)** | The natural logarithm of the odds; the scale on which logistic regression is linear. |
| **Logit link** | The transformation that connects a probability to a linear predictor on the log-odds scale. |
| **Odds ratio** | The multiplicative change in the odds associated with a one-unit increase in a predictor. |
| **Simple logistic regression** | A logistic model with a single predictor of a binary outcome. |
| **Predicted probability** | The fitted chance of the event, obtained by mapping the linear predictor back through the logistic function. |
